name: Potassium
name_origin: English 'potassium' derives from 'potash' (pot ashes), the substance from which it was first isolated. The symbol K comes from the Latin 'kalium', from Arabic 'al-qalyah' meaning 'plant ashes'.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Potassium is the seventh most abundant element in Earth's crust (~2.1% by mass) and the eighth most abundant element overall. It does not occur as a free metal in nature due to its high reactivity, but is widespread in minerals such as sylvite, carnallite, and feldspar.

compounds.name: Potassium chloride
compounds.summary: A naturally occurring salt used as a fertilizer, salt substitute, and in medical applications.

compounds.name: Potassium hydroxide
compounds.summary: A strong caustic base used in the manufacture of soft soaps, biodiesel, and as an electrolyte in alkaline batteries.

compounds.name: Potassium nitrate (saltpeter)
compounds.summary: A key oxidizer in gunpowder, fireworks, and a common fertilizer ingredient.

compounds.name: Potassium carbonate (potash)
compounds.summary: Used in glass and soap manufacturing and as a mild drying agent.

compounds.name: Potassium permanganate
compounds.summary: A vivid purple oxidizing agent used in water treatment, disinfection, and organic chemistry.

uses: Potash fertilizers for agriculture (over 90% of mined potassium)
NaK alloy as a heat-transfer fluid in some experimental nuclear reactors
Potassium hydroxide in biodiesel production and soft soap manufacture
Potassium nitrate in gunpowder, pyrotechnics, and food preservation
Potassium chloride as a sodium-free dietary salt substitute
Potassium superoxide (KO2) in self-contained breathing apparatus to generate O2
reactivity_summary: Potassium is an extremely reactive alkali metal that reacts vigorously and exothermically with water to produce hydrogen gas, often igniting the hydrogen with a lilac flame. It oxidizes rapidly in air and must be stored under mineral oil or inert gas. Potassium is more reactive than sodium and reacts violently with halogens and acids.
isotopes_summary: Potassium has three naturally occurring isotopes: 39K (~93.26%), 41K (~6.73%), and the long-lived radioactive 40K (~0.012%, half-life 1.25 billion years). 40K is the largest source of natural radioactivity in animals, including humans, and its decay to 40Ar is the basis for potassium-argon geological dating.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
